patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s primary caregiver, bringing your 2-year-old son for poor feeding and poor weight gain. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “Feeding has been a struggle—he gets tired quickly and doesn’t finish bottles, and he isn’t gaining weight.” Volunteer early: coughing/choking a bit with drinking, feels “floppy,” and he’s behind—still not sitting on his own. correct_diagnosis.primary: Hypomyelinating leukodystrophy
required_evidence: History of feeding difficulties with neurodevelopmental concern (e.g., hypotonia, delayed milestones) consistent with leukodystrophy presentation
Brain MRI pattern consistent with hypomyelination (diffuse, persistent T2 hyperintensity of white matter with relative lack of myelination for age)
Exclusion of common acquired causes of feeding difficulty (e.g., infection, dehydration, electrolyte derangement) based on clinical assessment and basic labs
Neurology evaluation and genetic testing plan for hypomyelinating leukodystrophy spectrum (e.g., PLP1, POLR3A/B, TUBB4A) or leukodystrophy gene panel

expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically within reference range; may be low with poor intake/vomiting if present
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically within reference range; may be low if dehydration/metabolic acidosis from poor intake
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: May be mildly elevated if dehydration from inadequate intake; otherwise within reference range

medications: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy indicated for hypomyelinating leukodystrophy; avoid empiric antibiotics unless clear infection
non_pharmacological: Assess airway protection and aspiration risk; bedside swallow evaluation and refer to speech-language pathology for formal feeding/swallow assessment
Nutrition optimization: dietitian referral; consider high-calorie feeds and thickened feeds if recommended by swallow study
Hydration support: oral rehydration if safe; consider IV fluids if unable to maintain hydration orally
Evaluate for gastroesophageal reflux and constipation contributing to feeding difficulty; manage per pediatric guidelines (non-pharm first, then targeted therapy if clinically indicated)
Multidisciplinary care: pediatric neurology referral, genetics referral for confirmatory testing and counseling, PT/OT for motor support
Discuss feeding tube options (e.g., NG short-term, gastrostomy if chronic unsafe/insufficient intake) if persistent failure to thrive or aspiration risk
Arrange brain MRI review/ordering if not already done to document hypomyelination pattern
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for feeding/weight check (earlier in 48-72 hours if intake remains poor), and neurology/genetics follow-up as soon as available (typically within 2-4 weeks)

must_include: Hypomyelinating leukodystrophy is a disorder of brain white matter where myelin does not form normally
Feeding difficulties can occur due to neurologic control of swallowing/coordination and low tone
Diagnosis is supported by MRI findings and confirmed/characterized with genetic testing
Course and prognosis vary by genetic subtype; uncertainty should be acknowledged
There is no definitive cure; care focuses on symptom management, nutrition, safety, and development
Plan for swallow evaluation and nutrition support to reduce aspiration risk and support growth
When to seek urgent care: signs of dehydration, choking/aspiration, breathing difficulty, lethargy, persistent vomiting, or decreased urine output
Validate caregiver concerns and assess goals (growth, comfort, safety, development)
Discuss aspiration risk and potential need for temporary or longer-term tube feeding if oral intake is unsafe/insufficient
Review allergy history (rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate) and reassure that antibiotics will be used only when indicated
Safe feeding strategies pending swallow guidance (upright positioning, paced feeds, monitoring for coughing/wet voice)
Importance of regular weight checks and tracking intake/urine output
Coordination of multidisciplinary services and community supports/early intervention

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome, postsynaptic (AChR deficiency/rapsyn/DOK7 spectrum)
required_evidence: History of fatigable weakness with fluctuation (worse with exertion; improves with rest)
Predominantly ocular/bulbar and/or proximal limb weakness without sensory loss (e.g., ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, exertional dyspnea)
Electrodiagnostic evidence of neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)

Absence of features supporting alternative diagnoses: no sensory neuropathy signs (neuronopathy), no myopathic pattern with elevated CK as primary driver, no fixed distal pattern typical of distal hereditary motor neuropathy/distal myopathy
Genetic confirmation of a postsynaptic CMS-related gene variant (e.g., CHRNE, RAPSN, DOK7) OR strong clinical/EDX phenotype consistent with postsynaptic CMS pending genetics

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected to explain weakness)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be low if chronic hypoventilation with respiratory acidosis compensation is not captured on CMP (ABG not provided)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 4-6 hours while awake; titrate to effect (usual range 30-60 mg per dose; max guided by tolerability)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily OR 4 mg PO twice daily; titrate as tolerated (particularly helpful in some postsynaptic CMS subtypes such as DOK7/rapsyn)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily; titrate gradually as tolerated (specialist-directed; avoid if seizure risk)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for CMS subtype confirmation and genotype-guided therapy; arrange genetic testing if not already done
Baseline and periodic respiratory assessment (FVC sitting/supine if available; assess for nocturnal hypoventilation symptoms); low threshold for sleep study if morning headaches/daytime somnolence
Swallow evaluation if bulbar symptoms; aspiration precautions and diet texture modification as indicated
Physical therapy with energy conservation strategies; avoid overexertion that triggers prolonged fatigue
Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium, certain beta-blockers) unless essential and monitored
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom response and adverse effects; earlier (within 48-72 hours) if worsening bulbar/respiratory symptoms

must_include: CMS is a genetic disorder affecting neuromuscular junction signaling (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with fatigue; sensory function is typically preserved
Subtype matters because some CMS forms respond differently to medications
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness
How to take pyridostigmine (timing with activities/meals) and common side effects (cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions) and when to call for help
Some CMS subtypes may not benefit from pyridostigmine and may improve with beta-2 agonists or amifampridine; therapy will be adjusted based on response/genetics
Emergency warning signs: increasing shortness of breath, inability to swallow/handle secretions, choking/aspiration, rapidly worsening weakness
Discuss inheritance and offer genetic counseling for family planning
Set expectations: improvement may be partial and requires titration; avoid abrupt medication changes without guidance
Review medications to avoid that can worsen weakness; advise patient to inform all clinicians of CMS diagnosis
Energy conservation and pacing; prioritize activities when medication effect is strongest
Aspiration precautions if bulbar symptoms; consider smaller meals and upright posture during/after eating
Infection and sleep deprivation can worsen symptoms; plan early evaluation if illness triggers deterioration

Critical safety check before prescribing; ensure no high-risk combinations (e.g., QT-prolonging drugs if considering beta-2 agonists/amifampridine, cholinergic additive effects if pyridostigmine).

Critical safety check before prescribing; screen for conditions affecting medication choice (e.g., asthma/COPD secretion burden for pyridostigmine, arrhythmia for beta-2 agonists, seizure history for amifampridine).

nl_assertions: The agent must differentiate CMS from autoimmune myasthenia gravis and avoid recommending immunosuppression or thymectomy as routine CMS therapy.
The agent must explicitly state that CMS is genetic and that subtype/genetics influence medication response and selection.

The agent must ask about fluctuating fatigable weakness (worse with exertion, improves with rest), ocular symptoms (ptosis/diplopia), bulbar symptoms (dysphagia, nasal speech, choking), and respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, orthopnea, weak cough).

The agent must screen for emergency features (increasing shortness of breath, inability to swallow/handle secretions, aspiration/choking, rapidly worsening generalized weakness) and provide clear instructions to seek urgent/emergency care if present.
Before any prescribing action, the agent must perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; missing any of these is a critical failure.

If prescribing pyridostigmine, the agent must counsel on timing with activities/meals, expected onset/duration conceptually, and common side effects (abdominal cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions/sweating) and when to call for help (severe diarrhea, bradycardia/syncope, bronchospasm, worsening secretions/dyspnea).

The agent must acknowledge that some CMS subtypes may not benefit from pyridostigmine and may worsen; it must mention that beta-2 agonists and/or amifampridine may be considered depending on subtype and specialist guidance.
The agent must discuss inheritance and offer/referral for genetic counseling for patient and family planning.
The agent must set expectations that improvement may be partial and requires titration; advise against abrupt medication changes without clinician guidance.

The agent must review and advise avoidance/caution with medications that can worsen neuromuscular weakness (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium, some beta-blockers, certain anesthetics/neuromuscular blockers) and instruct the patient to inform all clinicians of the CMS diagnosis.

The agent must provide lifestyle guidance: pacing/energy conservation, planning activities when medication effect is strongest, infection/sleep deprivation as triggers with early evaluation if deterioration, and aspiration precautions if bulbar symptoms (small meals, upright posture during/after eating).
The agent’s plan must be appropriate for L1 difficulty: clear, non-technical explanations, minimal but correct medication plan, and straightforward follow-up and safety netting.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (hemolytic anemia due to red cell membrane cation leak)

required_evidence: Chronic or intermittent hemolytic anemia with reticulocytosis (elevated reticulocyte % and absolute reticulocyte count)

Exclusion of more common causes of anemia using available indices (e.g., no microcytosis pattern suggestive of iron deficiency/thalassemia)
Clinical context consistent with inherited hemolysis (lifelong anemia/jaundice, family history, episodic hemolysis; splenomegaly may be present)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low (anemia; severity variable)

Transfuse packed RBCs only if symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise; use standard transfusion thresholds individualized to symptoms/comorbidity
Screen for and manage complications of chronic hemolysis (pigment gallstones; consider evaluation if biliary symptoms)
Update vaccinations per age/standard schedule; if splenectomy ever contemplated, ensure pre-splenectomy immunizations and thrombosis plan via hematology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2–4 weeks; sooner (within 24–72 hours) if worsening fatigue, dyspnea, jaundice, dark urine, or symptomatic anemia

comorbidities: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) with recurrent epistaxis
Iron-deficiency anemia (secondary to chronic mucosal bleeding)
confounders: Chronic diarrhea previously attributed to irritable bowel syndrome or infectious gastroenteritis
Pantoprazole use can raise chromogranin A and complicate neuroendocrine tumor biomarker interpretation
Recurrent epistaxis and chronic iron-deficiency anemia from HHT may distract from volume depletion due to secretory diarrhea
Hypokalemia and metabolic acidosis could be misattributed to laxative abuse or renal tubular acidosis without stool studies

correct_diagnosis.primary: VIPoma (vasoactive intestinal peptide–secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; WDHA syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting VIP (VIP-secreting pNET)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum VIP (typically >200 pg/mL) in the setting of chronic secretory watery diarrhea
Hypokalemia from gastrointestinal potassium loss
Non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) consistent with diarrheal bicarbonate loss
Volume depletion/azotemia possible (elevated BUN/creatinine) with ongoing high-volume diarrhea

Exclusion of alternative causes of episodic swelling (hereditary angioedema), lysosomal storage disease (Sandhoff), severe hypertriglyceridemia syndrome (familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency), and hypercalcemia syndrome (primary parathyroid hyperplasia)

expected_lab_results.Serum VIP: Elevated (classically >200 pg/mL; often markedly high) on a fasting sample
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (HCO3-): Low (non–anion gap metabolic acidosis from diarrhea)
expected_lab_results.Chloride (Cl-): Often elevated relative to bicarbonate loss (hyperchloremia consistent with non–anion gap acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Sodium (Na+): Often low-normal to mildly low due to volume depletion; may be normal depending on intake/repletion
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: May be elevated if dehydrated/AKI from volume loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated with dehydration (prerenal azotemia)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): May be elevated (VIP can cause hyperglycemia); can be normal

medications: 0.9% Sodium chloride IV bolus 20 mL/kg then maintenance per vitals/urine output
Potassium chloride IV 10-20 mEq/hour with continuous ECG monitoring if severe hypokalemia; or PO KCl 40-100 mEq/day divided if mild/moderate and able to tolerate
Octreotide 50-100 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (titrate to diarrhea control); consider long-acting release after stabilization
Magnesium sulfate IV as needed if hypomagnesemia suspected/confirmed (replace per institutional protocol)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; strict input/output, daily weights, and stool volume quantification
Frequent electrolyte monitoring and repletion (K+, HCO3-/acid-base, renal function) until stable

Avoid dehydration triggers; oral rehydration solution when able; consider inpatient admission if ongoing high-volume diarrhea or significant electrolyte derangements
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (inpatient or urgent outpatient depending on severity) for symptom response and repeat electrolytes; specialty follow-up within 1-2 weeks for tumor localization/staging and definitive treatment planning

must_include: VIPoma is a rare hormone-secreting neuroendocrine tumor that causes large-volume watery diarrhea
Diarrhea leads to low potassium and acid-base imbalance (low bicarbonate), which can be dangerous
High fasting VIP level supports the diagnosis; additional imaging is needed to locate/stage the tumor
Immediate priorities are IV fluids and electrolyte replacement to prevent arrhythmias and kidney injury
Octreotide (somatostatin analog) reduces VIP secretion and diarrhea; dosing may be adjusted based on response
Definitive management often involves tumor-directed therapy (surgery if feasible; other therapies if spread)
Review penicillin allergy and confirm no planned antibiotic exposure without alternatives
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care: fainting, severe weakness, palpitations, reduced urination, confusion, or inability to keep fluids down
Set expectations that symptom control may improve quickly with octreotide and rehydration, but tumor workup is still necessary
Maintain hydration with oral rehydration solutions when possible; avoid plain water only if large losses (needs electrolytes)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to assess severity of secretory diarrhea, dehydration, electrolyte-related symptoms, and rule out common causes; confirm medication use and allergy history.

Order initial stabilization/diagnostic labs for watery diarrhea and suspected VIPoma: BMP (K, Na, Cr), Mg, phosphate, serum bicarbonate/CO2, venous/arterial blood gas if ill, glucose, CBC; stool studies as appropriate; fasting VIP level; consider stool osmotic gap if available.
Interpret labs to identify hypokalemia, metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate), renal injury/dehydration, and elevated fasting VIP supporting VIPoma.

Make working diagnosis of VIPoma with secretory diarrhea and electrolyte/acid-base derangements; document need for imaging to localize/stage (CT/MRI pancreas, somatostatin receptor imaging) and consider differential if VIP not elevated.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing; verify reported penicillin allergy details and ensure no planned antibiotic exposure without alternatives.

Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing; screen for interactions with octreotide and electrolyte replacement (e.g., glucose effects, bradycardia risk with beta-blockers, QT/arrhythmia context).

Initiate treatment: aggressive IV fluids and electrolyte replacement (especially potassium, magnesium) and start octreotide to reduce VIP secretion/diarrhea; tailor dosing to response and severity; avoid unsafe antidiarrheal masking.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment, address concerns (allergy review and red flags), and lifestyle guidance (oral rehydration, tracking outputs, avoid OTC antidiarrheals unless advised).
Arrange urgent follow-up/transfer as indicated, and specialty referral (endocrinology/oncology/surgery) plus imaging to localize/stage; plan lab rechecks for K, bicarbonate, renal function.
Close encounter after ensuring safety netting, follow-up plan, and patient understanding.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old warehouse shift supervisor. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “I’ve got this hard, tight patch of skin that keeps spreading, and my fingers go numb and change color in the cold.” Early on, volunteer only: shiny, stuck-down hard patch on forearm; fingers turn white/blue/numb with cold/stress then throb when warm; morning hand puffiness/rings tight; tight finger skin makes bending harder.

If asked, add: patch has dark edge/light center, not itchy; daily heartburn and occasional food sticking; more short of breath on stairs; fingertip cracks/slow healing; wrist/finger aches and morning stiffness; dry mouth and smaller mouth opening; tiny red “dot” spots; hands unusually cold.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: night cough/reflux, loose stools/bloating/weight loss, recent higher home BP with headaches, knuckle/elbow darkening, finger weakness.

Resist disclosing painful sex/vaginal dryness, embarrassment/social avoidance, skipping meals due to swallowing fear unless trust built.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s chilblains/allergy/“arthritis eating bones,” fear tests are unaffordable, prefer quick fixes, doubt generics and fear brand costs, avoid follow-ups due to missing shifts. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay cautious.

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO once daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO once daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
confounders: Ibrutinib-associated bruising and rash can mimic inflammatory/vasculitic skin changes
Aicardi-Goutieres syndrome with chronic interferon-driven inflammation can elevate ESR/CRP and cause cutaneous findings
Possible early scleroderma renal involvement vs medication-related renal function fluctuation (mild creatinine elevation with proteinuria)
Raynaud-like vasospasm from cold exposure or nicotine can be mistaken for systemic sclerosis vascular disease
Gastroesophageal reflux symptoms (on PPI) may be attributed to systemic sclerosis GI involvement

correct_diagnosis.primary: Scleroderma (localized scleroderma/morphea presenting as a cutaneous sclerotic plaque)
acceptable_alternatives: Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (if systemic features are present)
required_evidence: Indurated/sclerotic plaque with skin thickening and bound-down texture on exam
Lesion morphology compatible with morphea (e.g., ivory center with violaceous/lilac border early) and absence of infectious features

Targeted review of systems for systemic sclerosis negative or not supportive (Raynaud phenomenon, digital ulcers, dysphagia/GERD, dyspnea, inflammatory arthritis) OR if positive, prompts systemic sclerosis workup
Consider skin biopsy demonstrating dermal sclerosis with thickened collagen bundles and loss of adnexal structures if diagnosis uncertain

Differential exclusion based on clinical pattern: no acral cold-induced painful papules/ulcers typical of chilblain lupus; no childhood-onset progressive osteolysis/arthropathy pattern; no systemic autoinflammatory syndrome features

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal in isolated cutaneous disease; anemia would be atypical and should prompt alternate/secondary evaluation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal; leukocytosis would suggest infection/inflammation from another cause
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (renal involvement not expected in localized morphea); elevation would prompt evaluation for systemic disease/other causes

medications: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment apply thin layer to plaque(s) topically twice daily for 2-4 weeks, then reassess/taper
Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment apply to affected area topically twice daily (steroid-sparing option for maintenance or sensitive sites)
non_pharmacological: Assess extent and depth (photographs, body surface area; evaluate for linear/deep involvement and functional limitation)
Moisturization and gentle skin care; avoid trauma to affected area
Physical/occupational therapy and range-of-motion exercises if lesion crosses joints or causes restriction
Dermatology referral; consider biopsy if atypical features or diagnostic uncertainty

If rapidly progressive, extensive, linear, or deep morphea: plan escalation (specialist-directed) to phototherapy (UVA1 or NB-UVB) and/or systemic therapy (e.g., methotrexate with short course systemic corticosteroids) after appropriate screening and monitoring
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Re-evaluate in 4-6 weeks to assess response and progression; sooner (2-4 weeks) if rapidly expanding, painful, ulcerating, or function-limiting

must_include: Explain that the finding is most consistent with localized scleroderma (morphea), a condition causing localized skin thickening/scarring
Clarify difference between localized morphea and systemic sclerosis, and that systemic symptoms will be screened for
Discuss expected course (often slowly progressive then stabilizes) and importance of monitoring for spread or functional impact
Goal is to reduce inflammation and prevent progression/contracture; existing pigment/texture changes may take time to improve
How to apply high-potency topical steroid safely (thin layer, limited duration, avoid face/groin unless directed)
Potential adverse effects (skin atrophy, striae, telangiectasia) and when to stop/seek care
Review current medication (ibrutinib) and advise to inform oncology/hematology about new skin findings and any new topical/systemic therapies
Red flags requiring earlier evaluation: rapid enlargement, new lesions, ulceration, severe pain, joint stiffness/limited motion, Raynaud symptoms, shortness of breath, dysphagia
Skin care: moisturize, avoid irritants/trauma, protect affected area
If systemic features suspected/confirmed: counsel on cold avoidance and smoking cessation for Raynaud risk reduction
Importance of follow-up and photographing lesions to track changes

If systemic features are present/uncertain, order screening tests to evaluate for systemic sclerosis/overlap and organ involvement (e.g., ANA with reflex/ENA including anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70, anti-RNA pol III; CBC/CMP; urinalysis; consider ESR/CRP).
Review ordered test results (if any) to support localized morphea vs systemic sclerosis/overlap and to guide referral/monitoring intensity.
State most likely diagnosis (localized scleroderma/morphea) when presentation fits; document differential and triggers for escalation to systemic sclerosis evaluation.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication (e.g., high-potency topical corticosteroid).

Critical safety check before prescribing; consider interaction/bleeding risk context with ibrutinib if considering systemic agents; verify topical therapy compatibility and counsel to coordinate with oncology.

Initiate appropriate first-line therapy for localized morphea (e.g., high-potency topical corticosteroid) and provide safe-use instructions; avoid systemic immunosuppression unless indicated and coordinated.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis vs systemic sclerosis, treatment goals and adverse effects, address ibrutinib coordination and red flags, and provide lifestyle/skin care guidance.
Arrange reassessment to monitor response/progression, functional impact, and need for dermatology/rheumatology referral (earlier if red flags).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had a mild fever and this itchy rash for a couple days.” Volunteer early: low-grade on/off fever, itchy red rash that began on your chest and spread to arms/legs, red/irritated eyes, and feeling more tired. If asked about other symptoms, add: aching joints (wrists/ankles), mild headache behind the eyes, muscle aches, swollen/tender neck glands, slightly decreased appetite; deny vomiting or severe belly pain. Do not mention unless specifically asked about travel/exposures/reproductive/sexual history: you traveled to a mosquito-heavy area ~10–11 days ago and had many bites; you’re trying to get pregnant and not using reliable birth control; no unusual bleeding/bruising. If asked about sex, be hesitant but admit unprotected sex after returning and worry about passing something to your partner. Express misconceptions: you wonder about dengue, a new OTC allergy med reaction, or measles. If shown empathy, thank them and share cost/work concerns about testing, follow-up ultrasounds, and missing work.

Sertraline 50 mg PO daily

confounders: Recent travel to mosquito-endemic area with multiple Aedes-borne viruses (dengue/chikungunya overlap)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Zika virus infectious disease
acceptable_alternatives: Suspected Zika virus disease (pending confirmatory testing)
required_evidence: Compatible acute illness (e.g., low-grade fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgia, conjunctivitis) within 2 weeks of possible mosquito exposure or sexual exposure in an area with Zika risk
Positive Zika virus NAAT (RT-PCR) in serum collected during acute phase OR high clinical suspicion with appropriate exposure while awaiting results
Absence of severe bacterial features (e.g., focal infection, hemodynamic instability) and no alternative diagnosis that better explains symptoms

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly low (possible mild leukopenia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal to mildly low (not markedly decreased)
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - Pregnancy test (hCG): Obtain for all patients of childbearing potential; result may be positive or negative (must be checked to guide counseling)
expected_lab_results.Zika virus NAAT (RT-PCR) - Serum: Positive if drawn during early acute illness (generally within ~7 days of symptom onset); may be negative if collected later despite infection

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care: rest, oral hydration, cool compresses for fever
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk; avoid aspirin in children/adolescents (Reye risk)
Prevent mosquito bites during first week of illness (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets, staying indoors) to reduce onward transmission
Sexual transmission prevention: use condoms/abstinence after possible Zika exposure (men: at least 3 months; women: at least 2 months) and throughout pregnancy if pregnant
If pregnant or pregnancy possible: counsel on fetal risks (congenital Zika syndrome) and arrange prompt obstetric/MFM follow-up and serial fetal ultrasound per local guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours if worsening symptoms; otherwise follow up in 3-7 days for test results and counseling. Immediate evaluation for neurologic symptoms (weakness, paresthesias) suggestive of Guillain-Barré syndrome.

must_include: Zika is a viral infection usually spread by mosquitoes and can also spread through sex
Illness is often mild and self-limited (typically days to ~1 week)
Testing interpretation: RT-PCR is most useful early; a negative later test does not fully exclude infection
No specific antiviral therapy; treatment is supportive
Use acetaminophen for fever/pain
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded
Pregnancy implications: if pregnant or trying to conceive, discuss congenital Zika risks and need for pregnancy testing and obstetric follow-up
Red flags requiring urgent care: severe headache, bleeding, dehydration, confusion, shortness of breath, or new neurologic symptoms (possible Guillain-Barré)
Mosquito bite prevention measures during and after illness
Sexual transmission prevention timelines (men 3 months, women 2 months after exposure/symptom onset; throughout pregnancy if applicable)
Avoid travel to areas with Zika risk during pregnancy if possible and follow local public health guidance

Required safety check before recommending/prescribing symptomatic medications (e.g., acetaminophen).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., acetaminophen with other meds; avoid duplicate APAP products).
Provide supportive care plan; prioritize acetaminophen and hydration; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded.

Close encounter with safety-net instructions and confirmation of understanding.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I found a new lump in my neck and it’s making me nervous.” Early in the visit, volunteer: mild tightness when swallowing, fatigue for 2 months, and long-standing small bumps around your mouth/tongue you assumed were skin tags. If asked about details, say the neck lump has slowly enlarged over 6–8 weeks, you get hoarse by day’s end, more headaches, bloating/loose stools after meals, new dark spots on hands/face, heavier periods, and prior benign breast lump with recurrent tender lumpiness.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: big head/hat size, recurrent wart-like bumps on hands/feet, frequent heartburn/food sticking, past colon polyps removed, or prior thyroid nodules you didn’t follow up.

Resist/avoid genital bumps and postcoital bleeding unless the clinician asks directly and normalizes it; then disclose, embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: think it might be infection after a sore throat, fatigue from diabetes, fear of lymphoma due to night sweats, GI issues from gluten/lactose.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share insurance/time-off and genetic/life-insurance worries.

confounders: Autoimmune thyroiditis with subclinical hypothyroidism (can mimic thyroid malignancy symptoms and alters thyroid nodule interpretation)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome) with autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis) screening/assessment
acceptable_alternatives: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis) in a patient with Cowden syndrome
required_evidence: Known or strongly suspected Cowden syndrome/PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (personal or family history; mucocutaneous lesions; macrocephaly; hamartomas; prior PTEN mutation if available)
Thyroid evaluation in Cowden syndrome due to elevated lifetime thyroid cancer risk
Thyroid autoimmunity evidence (positive TPO and/or thyroglobulin antibodies) with thyroid function pattern consistent with euthyroid, subclinical hypothyroidism, or overt hypothyroidism
No evidence from provided labs alone to diagnose diabetes if glycemic indices are normal; if abnormal, classify per ADA criteria

expected_lab_results.TSH: Often mildly elevated if subclinical/overt hypothyroidism from Hashimoto thyroiditis; may be normal if euthyroid
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal in subclinical hypothyroidism; low in overt hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Thyroglobulin antibody: May be positive (supportive of autoimmune thyroiditis)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Expected normal (<5.7%) unless comorbid dysglycemia is present
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Expected normal (<100 mg/dL) unless comorbid dysglycemia is present
expected_lab_results.C-peptide (fasting): If glucose normal, typically normal; if hyperglycemia present, helps distinguish insulin deficiency (low) vs insulin resistance (normal/high)
expected_lab_results.GAD65 antibody: Expected negative unless autoimmune type 1 diabetes/LADA is present

medications: Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (or 25-50 mcg PO daily if older/cardiac risk) if TSH elevated with low Free T4 (overt hypothyroidism) or if persistent subclinical hypothyroidism with symptoms or TSH >=10 mIU/L
non_pharmacological: Thyroid cancer surveillance appropriate for Cowden syndrome: annual thyroid ultrasound starting at diagnosis (or in childhood per syndrome guidance), with endocrine referral
If euthyroid or mild subclinical hypothyroidism: monitor TSH/Free T4 every 6-12 months; treat based on thresholds/symptoms/pregnancy plans
Counsel on proper levothyroxine administration if prescribed (empty stomach, separate from iron/calcium by 4 hours)
Genetic counseling and syndrome-appropriate cancer surveillance coordination (breast/endometrial/colon/renal/dermatologic per PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome guidelines)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck TSH and Free T4 6-8 weeks after starting or changing levothyroxine; otherwise thyroid labs every 6-12 months with annual thyroid ultrasound

must_include: Cowden syndrome/PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome increases risk of thyroid disease and thyroid cancer
Current thyroid labs suggest autoimmune thyroiditis and determine whether thyroid function is normal, mildly low (subclinical), or low (overt)
These blood tests do not diagnose thyroid cancer; imaging surveillance is needed
When levothyroxine is indicated (overt hypothyroidism; persistent subclinical with symptoms or TSH >=10; individualized factors)
Expected benefits (normalize thyroid levels, improve symptoms) and that dose is adjusted by repeat labs
How to take levothyroxine correctly and common interactions (separate from calcium/iron; consistent timing)
Discuss cancer surveillance plan and rationale (annual thyroid ultrasound; specialist follow-up)
Review what symptoms should prompt earlier evaluation (neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, rapid growth, hyper/hypothyroid symptoms)
Acknowledge penicillin allergy and document it; clarify that proposed thyroid medication is unrelated
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and surveillance due to lifelong elevated cancer risk in Cowden syndrome
General metabolic health counseling (healthy diet, physical activity) and diabetes screening interpretation if A1c/FPG abnormal
Medication adherence and avoiding over-the-counter supplements that interfere with thyroid hormone absorption without discussing with clinician

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)

Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant, but variable)
Electrodiagnostic testing supportive (nerve conduction studies: demyelinating slow velocities in CMT1 vs reduced amplitudes in CMT2) OR genetic testing confirming a CMT-associated mutation
Exclusion of common acquired neuropathy mimics with available labs (diabetes, hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, inflammatory markers)

Course is usually gradual; severity varies even within families

key_points: CMT is an inherited peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness and sensory changes
Symptoms often include high arches/foot deformities, foot drop, and reduced reflexes
Diagnosis is supported by exam plus nerve conduction studies and/or genetic testing; routine labs help rule out mimics
Course is usually gradual; severity varies even within families
No cure currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Core therapies: PT/OT, bracing/orthotics, and foot/ankle support; surgery only for selected severe deformities
Neuropathic pain can be treated with agents like gabapentin; start low and titrate to effect/tolerability
Coordination with neurology and (if applicable) oncology is important due to medication-related neuropathy risks
Vincristine can cause severe/worsening neuropathy and is generally contraindicated/should be avoided in CMT when possible
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent evaluation: rapidly worsening weakness, new breathing/swallowing issues, sudden inability to walk, frequent falls
Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling/testing options
Foot care and fall prevention: proper footwear, home safety modifications, and assistive devices as needed
Regular low-impact exercise within tolerance to maintain strength and balance
Avoid or minimize exposure to neurotoxic medications and excessive alcohol; review new medications with clinicians

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT support specialist at a regional hospital. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “I keep catching my toes when I walk, and my feet feel weak.” Voluntarily add that you trip more on uneven ground/stairs, lift your knees higher to avoid toe drag, and it has slowly worsened over a couple of years. If asked for details, explain it’s mainly weakness in the front of the lower legs/ankles (trouble pulling feet up), both feet but one started first, calves look thinner, you fatigue on long walks, little/no pain, and no numbness/tingling. Do not mention falls unless directly asked; if asked, admit a couple and say it’s embarrassing. Do not bring up inherited worries unless asked about family history; then share concern but say you don’t want to alarm family. Mention back ache only if asked about other symptoms; you think it might be a pinched nerve or shoes/“bad ankles.” If asked about fears, admit you read about ALS online and are worried. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open. If costs are discussed, express worry about genetic testing/specialists and fear of losing work quickly.

Metformin ER 500 mg once daily with evening meal
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU daily

diagnosis.primary: distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset

correct_diagnosis.primary: Distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset (Udd distal myopathy; commonly TTN-related tibial muscular dystrophy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset
required_evidence: Adult-onset, slowly progressive weakness beginning in anterior tibialis (ankle dorsiflexion) with foot drop
Distal lower-limb weakness predominates initially; proximal strength relatively preserved early
No sensory loss on history/exam (argues against peripheral neuropathy)
Creatine kinase normal to mildly elevated (not markedly high as in inflammatory myopathy)
Exclude common mimics with available labs (thyroid disease, diabetes-related neuropathy risk factors)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (typically <2–3× upper limit of normal)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mild elevation (can be muscle-derived)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Usually normal or mild elevation (less than AST if muscle source)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal (no diabetes) or mildly elevated if comorbid; not diagnostic of the myopathy
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Normal (or mildly elevated if comorbid); not diagnostic of the myopathy

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for confirmation and counseling; consider genetic testing for TTN and other distal myopathy genes
Physical therapy focused on stretching, gentle strengthening, gait training, and fall-prevention strategies
Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) for foot drop; consider assistive device (cane) if unstable
Occupational therapy for energy conservation and home safety modifications
Screen for and address complications: falls risk assessment; evaluate for contractures; consider baseline functional measures
Avoid myotoxic agents where possible (e.g., unnecessary statin initiation; review current meds for myopathy risk)
Offer genetic counseling and discussion of familial implications
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-8 weeks to review lab results, functional status, and referrals; then every 3-6 months for progression and supportive needs

must_include: Condition is a hereditary distal muscle disease that often starts with anterior tibialis weakness causing foot drop
Course is typically slowly progressive; symptoms and function can be supported even though there is no curative medication
Labs may be normal or only mildly abnormal; diagnosis is based on pattern of weakness and specialist evaluation (often including genetic testing)
Main treatment is supportive: PT/OT, bracing (AFO), fall prevention, and symptom control
Set expectations: goals are maintaining mobility, reducing falls, and preserving independence
Plan for neuromuscular referral and possible genetic testing
Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling
Discuss safety at home and risk of falls; encourage reporting new weakness, frequent falls, or new symptoms
Clarify that penicillin allergy is noted and will be avoided if antibiotics are needed
Encourage regular low-to-moderate intensity activity within tolerance; avoid overexertion that triggers prolonged soreness/weakness
Footwear and home modifications to reduce tripping (remove loose rugs, adequate lighting)
If diabetes or thyroid abnormalities are found, treating them is important but does not replace neuromuscular management

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset: onset/progression of foot drop, falls, distribution (distal vs proximal), symmetry, pain/cramps, sensory symptoms (should be absent), family history, triggers, and functional impact.

Screen for alternative/urgent etiologies and comorbid contributors: back pain/radicular symptoms, bowel/bladder changes, rapid progression, upper motor neuron signs, systemic symptoms, toxin/medication exposures (statins, steroids), alcohol use, diabetes/thyroid history.

Order baseline evaluation to support differential and exclude common mimics/complicators (e.g., CK, TSH, HbA1c/fasting glucose, CMP, CBC, B12); acknowledge that labs may be normal or mildly abnormal in distal myopathies.
Review labs to ensure no reversible metabolic cause is missed and to set expectations that normal/mild results do not exclude distal myopathy.

Synthesize pattern (anterior tibialis weakness/foot drop, distal > proximal, minimal sensory findings, slow progression, possible family history) consistent with distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset; document differential and need for neuromuscular confirmation/genetic testing.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, typical course, and that diagnosis is pattern-based with specialist evaluation (often genetic testing).
Explain treatment: supportive care (PT/OT, AFO bracing, fall prevention, symptom control), goals (maintain mobility/independence), and neuromuscular referral/genetic testing plan.
Address concerns: family implications and genetic counseling; fall-risk/home safety; red flags prompting reassessment; explicitly note penicillin allergy will be avoided if antibiotics needed.

Lifestyle counseling: low-to-moderate activity within tolerance, avoid overexertion causing prolonged soreness/weakness; footwear/home modifications; treat diabetes/thyroid issues if present but not a substitute for neuromuscular management.
Arrange neuromuscular referral and follow-up to review progression, falls, PT/OT/AFO implementation, and lab results; ensure safety planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and plan documented.

Start with: “I’ve had a really high fever and I can’t stop coughing, and I’m getting more short of breath.” Volunteer only: sudden high fever 3 days, worsening dry cough, chills/shaking, body aches, extreme fatigue, short of breath walking across the room. If pressed specifically about birds/dead birds/live markets, admit cleaning a coop and handling a dead chicken without gloves; only later, reluctantly reveal you kept a live chicken in your bathroom that died and you hid it from family. oseltamivir 75 mg PO twice daily
acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
confounders: recent exposure to sick poultry at a live bird market (high-risk zoonotic exposure)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Avian influenza (suspected/confirmed influenza A with zoonotic bird exposure)
acceptable_alternatives: Sjogren syndrome with interstitial lung disease
required_evidence: Epidemiologic risk: recent direct exposure to poultry/wild birds or contaminated environments in an affected area
Acute influenza-like illness with prominent respiratory symptoms (e.g., fever, cough, dyspnea) and rapid clinical progression
Laboratory pattern supportive of influenza: leukopenia and/or lymphopenia (not required but supportive)
Confirmatory testing when available: influenza A PCR; public health notification and subtyping for avian strain as indicated
Assessment for severe disease: hypoxemia, tachypnea, respiratory distress, or radiographic pneumonia/ARDS (if imaging available)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to low (leukopenia can be present in avian influenza); markedly high WBC would suggest bacterial pneumonia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils (absolute): Normal to mildly elevated early; significant neutrophilia would favor bacterial superinfection (e.g., staphylococcal pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Lymphocytes (absolute): Low (lymphopenia is common in severe influenza/avian influenza)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to low (thrombocytopenia can occur in severe viral infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Normal to mildly low (can be low with systemic illness/SIADH but nonspecific)

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking IT project manager. Start with: “I keep getting this tight pressure in my chest when I walk up stairs, and it scares me.” Early, volunteer: heavy tight pressure mid-chest with exertion (stairs), relieved by stopping/rest; mild shortness of breath with it; unusually tired for 2 weeks. If asked about details, add: sometimes radiates to left shoulder/jaw; triggered by cold air, stress, rushing; a few episodes woke you at night and you had to sit up; worst episodes cause sweating and nausea; ankles puffy by evening; occasional pounding/skipping heartbeat for seconds. Do NOT volunteer unless directly asked: needing more pillows, nocturia, recent weight gain, “indigestion” pressure after exertion, gradual decline in exercise tolerance. Be resistant/guarded if asked about substances/med adherence: minimize/hesitate about using a friend’s “little blue pills,” weekend cocaine, and skipping heart/cholesterol meds; worry about it being “in the chart.” Offer misconceptions: think it might be chest infection/pneumonia (cough, one feverish night/chills), lung issue (sharp pain with deep breath), or anxiety/asthma (panic breathing). If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and ask about costs/insurance, time off work, and preference for supplements over lifelong expensive meds.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Coronary artery disease (autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype) with suspected stable ischemic heart disease (no evidence of acute myocardial infarction on serial hs-troponin)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial hypercholesterolemia (heterozygous) with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
Stable angina due to coronary artery disease
required_evidence: Atherogenic lipid profile consistent with inherited hypercholesterolemia (markedly elevated LDL-C and/or ApoB, often elevated Lp(a))
Family history suggestive of autosomal dominant premature ASCVD and/or known familial hypercholesterolemia
No dynamic rise/fall in high-sensitivity troponin I between 0 and 3 hours (argues against acute myocardial infarction)
Clinical context consistent with coronary artery disease rather than primary pulmonary process (no lab evidence provided supporting pneumonia/organizing pneumonia/PAH)

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often markedly high, e.g., >240 mg/dL; may be >300 mg/dL in familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.LDL-C: Markedly elevated (often >=190 mg/dL in familial hypercholesterolemia; higher if untreated)
expected_lab_results.HDL-C: Normal to low (not diagnostic; may be low as an additional risk factor)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Normal to mildly elevated (typically not the primary abnormality in familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): Often elevated (independent ASCVD risk enhancer; may be >=50 mg/dL or >=125 nmol/L depending on units)
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Elevated (reflecting increased atherogenic particle number; commonly >130 mg/dL in severe hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (0 hr): Within reference range or minimally elevated without ischemic pattern; not diagnostic of MI alone
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (3 hr): No significant delta from 0 hr (no rise/fall meeting MI criteria)

Rosuvastatin 20 mg PO daily (high-intensity; avoid simvastatin due to prior myalgias)

Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL as needed for chest pain (may repeat every 5 minutes up to 3 doses; seek emergency care if pain persists)
non_pharmacological: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH; reduce saturated fat; increase soluble fiber)

Weight management and blood pressure optimization
Smoking cessation if applicable; avoid secondhand smoke
Discuss cascade screening for first-degree relatives for familial hypercholesterolemia and Lp(a)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting lipid panel and ApoB in 4-12 weeks after therapy initiation/intensification; earlier review (within 1-2 weeks) if ongoing chest pain or medication intolerance

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit chest pain history (OPQRST), exertional pattern, associated symptoms, prior CAD events, family history (autosomal dominant pattern), current meds, prior statin intolerance details, bleeding history, and pregnancy status if relevant.

Order/confirm key tests for CAD risk stratification and safe lipid-lowering therapy: fasting lipid panel (LDL-C, non-HDL), ApoB, Lp(a) if available, A1c, CMP/LFTs, CK if symptomatic, baseline ECG and serial troponins if acute symptoms are present.
Review results to support diagnosis, rule out acute MI (serial troponins without significant delta), and establish baseline LDL/ApoB for treatment targets.

Explain diagnosis in plain language, including plaque pathophysiology, inherited risk pattern, and meaning of serial troponins; discuss urgency/red flags despite low likelihood of MI today.
Perform mandatory allergy check before prescribing (e.g., aspirin/NSAID allergy, statin reactions, nitrate allergy).
Check for clinically relevant drug interactions prior to prescribing (e.g., nitrates with PDE-5 inhibitors; statins with strong CYP inhibitors; antiplatelets/anticoagulants).

Initiate evidence-based CAD secondary prevention tailored to statin myalgia history: alternative high-intensity statin as tolerated (e.g., rosuvastatin/atorvastatin with monitoring), consider add-on ezetimibe and/or PCSK9 inhibitor pathway if LDL/ApoB goals not met or intolerance persists; prescribe nitroglycerin with clear emergency instructions; consider aspirin only after contraindication/bleeding risk assessment and shared decision-making.

Explain treatment goals (aggressive LDL lowering), statin intolerance plan (what to do if myalgias recur), nitroglycerin use and when to call EMS, aspirin bleeding risk/contraindications, and PCSK9 injection option.
Provide lifestyle education emphasizing that FH often requires medication, adherence, follow-up labs, exercise/diet, and family cascade screening due to autosomal dominant inheritance.

Arrange follow-up for symptom reassessment and lipid response (e.g., 4–12 weeks after therapy change), repeat lipid panel/ApoB, monitor LFTs/CK if indicated, and referral to cardiology/lipid clinic as appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

general_concerns: Believes his balance problems are mainly from being "out of shape" and not trying hard enough, rather than a neurologic condition present since infancy.
Thinks cerebellar ataxia is always progressive like ALS or Parkinson’s and assumes he will inevitably end up in a wheelchair soon despite being told it’s nonprogressive.
Believes physical therapy will "wear out" his nerves and make his coordination permanently worse, so he avoids consistent exercises.
Assumes genetic conditions are contagious or can be "caught" by close contact, so he avoids dating and is reluctant to discuss family planning.
Thinks brain imaging (MRI) will expose him to dangerous radiation and cause infertility.
Believes alcohol "steadies" his gait and is safer than prescribed medications, so he underreports drinking.
economic_concerns: Worries that getting a formal neurologic diagnosis documented will make him lose his job or be denied future employment.
Assumes genetic testing will be automatically shared with insurers/employers and lead to higher premiums or loss of coverage.
Believes he can’t afford ongoing therapy/assistive devices and that there are no low-cost options or disability supports, so he delays follow-up.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old man. You speak vaguely and act evasive, giving short answers unless asked follow-ups. Start with: lifelong clumsiness, lately more stumbling and occasional slurred/slow speech when tired or stressed. Volunteer: unsteady since toddlerhood (walked late, fell a lot), trip on uneven ground/dark, shaky hands with precise tasks (buttons, writing, utensils), awkward running/turning, can’t do balance sports.

If asked, say it’s mostly stable over years, varies day to day; trouble with rapid alternating movements; eyes sometimes “jump” when shifting gaze; occasional coughing/choking with thin liquids if drinking fast; fatigue after longer walks; never rode a bike well; parents distantly related.

Withhold unless directly asked: school help for coordination/handwriting; avoiding stairs/handrails; ankle sprains; misjudging distance; worse with illness, sleep loss, alcohol; widened stance.

Resist discussing urinary urgency/leaks, social avoidance, falls, mood/anxiety—only admit if asked gently.

Express fears/misconceptions: MS/stroke, inner-ear dizziness, bruising/nosebleeds, big head syndrome, relative with SCA so you assume same. Worry diagnosis/genetic testing will reach employer/insurer; cost of therapy/devices.

If clinician is empathic, soften, admit embarrassment, ask about privacy and low-cost options.

diagnosis.primary: infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia of childhood
required_evidence: Onset in infancy/early childhood with delayed motor milestones and cerebellar signs (gait/truncal ataxia, limb dysmetria, intention tremor, nystagmus or dysarthria)
Clinical course is nonprogressive or very slowly changing over years (no relentless neurologic decline)
Family history compatible with autosomal recessive inheritance (affected siblings and/or consanguinity) or confirmatory genetic testing consistent with AR nonprogressive ataxia spectrum

No features suggesting alternative diagnoses: no stepwise or progressive cerebellar decline (argues against spinocerebellar ataxia and X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia), no syndromic mucocutaneous lesions/hamartomas or cancer predisposition (argues against Cowden disease), and no anemia with microcytosis/sideroblast pattern (argues against X-linked sideroblastic anemia)

expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (Hemoglobin): Within normal reference range (no anemia to suggest sideroblastic anemia)

medications: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy indicated; avoid empiric ataxia medications unless a specific comorbidity is identified
non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric neurology/genetics for confirmatory testing and counseling (including recurrence risk and family planning options)
Physical therapy for balance, gait training, and core strengthening; home exercise program
Occupational therapy for fine motor skills, adaptive equipment, and school accommodations
Speech-language therapy if dysarthria and/or swallowing concerns; screen for aspiration risk if symptoms
Fall-risk mitigation: assistive devices as needed (ankle-foot orthoses, walker), home safety evaluation, supervision planning
Educational support: individualized education plan/504 accommodations; neuropsychological evaluation if learning/attention concerns
Screen and manage associated issues as clinically indicated (vision assessment for nystagmus/strabismus; hearing if concerns)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 3-6 months to assess function, therapy response, and confirm nonprogressive course; sooner (4-6 weeks) after initiating therapies to coordinate services and safety planning

must_include: What cerebellar ataxia means (coordination/balance problem from cerebellar dysfunction)
Expected course: typically nonprogressive (not steadily worsening), but functional needs may change with growth
Rationale for diagnosis and why key alternatives are less likely (progressive ataxias, Cowden features, sideroblastic anemia)
Genetic/inheritance implications: autosomal recessive pattern and what that means for siblings/future pregnancies
No proven disease-modifying medication; focus is supportive/rehabilitative care
Therapy goals (improve safety, mobility, independence, communication/swallowing as needed)
Plan for referrals (neurology, genetics, PT/OT/SLP) and expected timelines
Safety planning for falls and school participation
Red flags prompting urgent reassessment (new regression, rapidly worsening balance, new weakness, seizures, severe headaches, swallowing/choking, or other new neurologic symptoms)
Discuss family questions about prognosis and recurrence risk; offer genetic counseling
Home and school safety modifications and appropriate activity selection (avoid high-risk activities without supervision)
Importance of adherence to therapy and regular follow-up
Encourage normal social participation with accommodations; connect to community resources/support groups if desired

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history to confirm a congenital/early-onset, typically nonprogressive cerebellar syndrome; characterize onset, trajectory, functional impact, and screen for red flags suggesting progressive/alternative etiologies.
Gather family history/consanguinity and prior testing to support autosomal recessive inheritance and to inform counseling and differential diagnosis.
Medication/supplement history and allergy history in anticipation of any symptomatic prescriptions and to meet required safety checks.

Order targeted evaluation if not already completed to exclude treatable/progressive mimics and common comorbid contributors (e.g., thyroid dysfunction, vitamin deficiencies) and to document baseline; avoid indiscriminate testing.
Review available results (prior MRI/neurology/genetics, labs) to support diagnosis rationale and explain why key alternatives are less likely.
State the diagnosis and clinical reasoning; confirm nonprogressive course and autosomal recessive inheritance implications.
Explain diagnosis in family-friendly language, expected course, differential rationale, and genetic implications; begin shared planning.
Explain treatment approach (supportive/rehabilitative), therapy goals, and referrals with timelines; discuss school/home safety and lifestyle accommodations.
Arrange follow-up with neurology/genetics and therapy services; set monitoring plan and red-flag return precautions.
Close after confirming understanding, addressing questions, and ensuring safety-netting and follow-up are in place.

nl_assertions: Uses developmentally appropriate, caregiver-facing language and avoids implying a cure or proven disease-modifying medication for this condition.
Explicitly defines cerebellar ataxia as a coordination/balance problem due to cerebellar dysfunction.
Communicates expected course as typically nonprogressive (not steadily worsening) while acknowledging functional needs can change with growth.

Provides a clear rationale for the diagnosis and explicitly addresses why key alternatives are less likely, including progressive ataxias; and mentions features that would suggest other syndromes (e.g., Cowden/PTEN features such as macrocephaly/mucocutaneous lesions; sideroblastic anemia features such as pallor/fatigue, abnormal CBC).

Elicits and documents red flags that would prompt urgent reassessment: new regression, rapid worsening balance, new weakness, seizures, severe headaches, swallowing/choking, or other new neurologic symptoms.

Discusses autosomal recessive inheritance: both parents typically carriers, 25% recurrence risk per pregnancy, and implications for siblings; offers genetic counseling and options for future pregnancies (carrier testing, prenatal/preimplantation testing where available).
Recommends supportive management: PT/OT/SLP as indicated, mobility aids, fall prevention, school accommodations/IEP/504, and adaptive strategies to maximize independence.
Addresses safety planning for falls and school participation, including appropriate activity selection and supervision; avoids overly restrictive recommendations without justification.
If any medication is proposed (e.g., for spasticity, tremor, drooling, sleep), performs CHECK_ALLERGY before PRESCRIBE; and performs interaction/contraindication checks when clinically relevant.
Does not prescribe potentially harmful or unproven therapies as disease-modifying treatment; avoids unnecessary antibiotics, steroids, or chelation without indication.
Engages in shared decision-making, checks understanding (teach-back), and responds to caregiver concerns about prognosis and daily function.
Plans follow-up with neurology and genetics, and specifies expected timelines for referrals and reassessment.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm early-onset nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia phenotype, exclude red flags for progressive disease, explain diagnosis and inheritance, initiate supportive referrals and safety plan without prescribing medications.
steps: ASK: focused ataxia history and red flags
ASK: family history/consanguinity and prior workup
ORDER_LAB: targeted screen for mimics if not already done (e.g., TSH, CMP, CBC, B12/Vit E as appropriate) and/or request prior MRI/genetics records
DIAGNOSE: infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis, course, differential rationale, and autosomal recessive inheritance/recurrence risk; offer genetic counseling
EDUCATE: supportive treatment plan; referrals to neurology, genetics, PT/OT/SLP; school/home safety and accommodations; red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: set timelines and monitoring plan

minimal_paths.description: Same as core path, plus symptomatic medication for a specific, documented problem (e.g., significant spasticity, sleep disturbance, drooling) with required safety checks prior to prescribing.
steps: ASK: focused ataxia history, red flags, and target symptom assessment
ORDER_LAB/GET_RESULTS: as indicated to rule out contributors relevant to symptom (e.g., constipation, anemia) and review prior workup
DIAGNOSE: infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia
PRESCRIBE: symptom-targeted medication with clear indication, dosing rationale, and monitoring plan
EDUCATE: diagnosis/course/differential/inheritance; supportive care and referrals; med counseling and adverse effects; red flags

acceptable_variants.description: If prior comprehensive workup is already documented (MRI, genetics, and relevant labs), the agent may skip ORDER_LAB and instead GET_RESULTS by requesting/confirming existing records before diagnosing and educating.
Agent may interleave education earlier (after preliminary history) to set expectations, then refine after results review, as long as all communication milestones are fully met by the end.

Agent may prioritize urgent safety-netting immediately if red flags are endorsed, recommending urgent evaluation; benchmark still credits correct red-flag identification and appropriate escalation even if full counseling is abbreviated.
